title: Spatially mapping thermal transport in graphene by an opto-thermal method
type1_summary: The repository contains multiple small, condition-specific MATLAB files labeled as formatted/pre-calculated temperature data. These are consistent with processed, figure-ready Raman thermometry outputs and likely direct inputs for plotting or FEM fitting.
type2_summary: No clear raw instrument-output files are present in the provided file list. The paper does describe raw Raman spectra/maps in the experiment, but the inspected files do not appear to be raw measurement exports.
classification_notes: The paper itself clearly contains raw Raman measurements, but the provided file list does not include obvious raw spectra or instrument exports. The MAT files are best interpreted as processed source data. The PDF is supplementary documentation rather than a dataset file.

file_classifications.paper_evidence: The paper states that datasets and code are available in a GitHub repository; this PDF is a Nature source-data supplement, likely accompanying the publication rather than raw instrument output.
file_classifications.file_evidence: PDF format, 7.1 MB, from nature_source_data. This is consistent with a supplementary source-data document or figure compilation, not a machine-readable dataset.
file_classifications.reasoning: This is not a data table or raw measurement file. It is best treated as documentation/supplementary material rather than Type 1 or Type 2 data.
file_classifications.ambiguity: Could contain source-data figures or captions, but without page-level inspection it cannot be treated as directly replot-ready data.

paper_analysis.summary: This paper presents a spatially resolved opto-thermal method to map the in-plane thermal conductivity of suspended graphene using confocal Raman thermometry combined with finite-element modeling. The authors demonstrate the method on pristine and He+-irradiated graphene membranes, finding strong spatial variations in thermal conductivity for pristine samples and a controlled reduction in conductivity with increasing defect density.
measurement_types: Raman thermometry / Raman peak shift mapping
He+-ion irradiation / focused ion beam defect engineering
electron microscopy (sample preparation / membrane patterning, mentioned in methods)

figures.description: Schematic of a suspended graphene membrane on a Si3N4 frame used for Raman thermometry and thermal mapping.
Workflow diagram showing the experimental Raman mapping/calibration steps and the FEM-based fitting procedure to obtain a thermal conductivity map.
Maps of the Lorentzian-fitted graphene 2D Raman peak position at several hotplate temperatures (T1–T7) under low laser power.
Temperature evolution of the 2D Raman peak frequency shown as a density plot, with example linear fits for selected points.
Spatial map of dω2D/dT across the membrane extracted from linear fits to the low-power temperature calibration data.
Raman 2D-peak position map acquired at high laser power (4 mW) at room temperature.
Derived temperature distribution obtained by combining the low-power calibration map with the high-power Raman map.
Example input thermal conductivity map used in FEM calculations.
Schematic of the FEM geometry and example temperature profiles for different laser spot positions on the membrane.
Calculated temperature map upon laser illumination generated from the FEM model.
Experimentally determined temperature map used as input for fitting.
Fitted temperature map from the FEM-based optimization.
Extracted thermal conductivity map of the pristine suspended graphene membrane.
figures.likely_source: processed data from experiment + FEM fit

figures.description: Converged absorption map after fitting.
figures.likely_source: processed data from experiment + FEM fit

figures.description: Histograms of fitted thermal conductivity for several hotplate temperatures.
Schematic of the He+-irradiated graphene membrane showing quadrants with different irradiation doses.
Experimentally observed temperature map of the defect-engineered graphene under laser illumination.
Fitted thermal conductivity map for the He+-irradiated membrane.
figures.likely_source: processed data from experiment + FEM fit

figures.description: Fitted temperature map corresponding to the defect-engineered sample.
Histogram of thermal conductivity values for the four irradiation quadrants.

paper_analysis.raw_measurement_details: Raw measurements include confocal Raman spectra/maps of suspended graphene acquired at low laser power for multiple hotplate temperatures and at high laser power for local heating, using a 532 nm laser on a WITec Alpha 300 R Raman microscope. The paper also reports He+-ion irradiation conditions for defect engineering, and mentions sample preparation/patterning of Si/Si3N4 membranes. The raw Raman spectra are fit to extract 2D-peak positions; the paper does not specify a raw file format.

dataset_characterization.data_availability_statement: The datasets analysed during the current study are available in the GitHub repository at https://github.com/MickaelPerrin74/ThermalconductivityMapping. The code used during the current study is also available in the same GitHub repository.
dataset_characterization.raw_data_description: The paper describes raw experimental data as confocal Raman spectra/maps collected pixel-by-pixel across suspended graphene membranes at different hotplate temperatures and laser powers. It does not explicitly state that the raw spectra files are deposited, nor does it specify file formats or directory structure.
dataset_characterization.processed_data_description: Processed data include Lorentzian-fitted 2D-peak position maps, dω2D/dT calibration maps, high-power Raman peak-position maps, derived temperature maps, fitted thermal conductivity maps, fitted absorption maps, and histograms of thermal conductivity for different temperatures or irradiation doses.
dataset_characterization.figure_data_description: Figure-specific data are clearly implied: the paper shows multiple maps and histograms that are likely reproduced from the repository as figure-ready source data. However, the text does not explicitly say 'source data' files are provided, only that the datasets analyzed are in GitHub.
dataset_characterization.scripts_description: Yes. The paper explicitly states that the code used during the current study is available in the GitHub repository. The computational workflow includes FEM calculations and an iterative fitting procedure, so scripts likely cover these analyses.
dataset_characterization.notes: The repository likely contains a mix of experimental Raman map data, derived/processed maps, and analysis/FEM code, but the paper does not enumerate exact filenames or formats. The main semantic distinction is that the thermal conductivity maps are not directly measured; they are inferred from Raman-derived temperature maps via FEM fitting. The paper also notes that the absorption is fixed at 2.7% for the first 100 fitting cycles and then fitted, which is important for understanding the processed model parameters. Supplementary information is mentioned, but its contents are not detailed in the provided text.

classification_prior.raw_data_evidence: The Methods and Results describe acquisition of Raman spectra/maps at low and high laser power, which are the primary measurements underlying the analysis.
classification_prior.source_data_evidence: The paper states that the datasets analysed are available in a GitHub repository and that the code is also available there, implying figure-ready processed data and analysis scripts.
classification_prior.data_availability_section_relevant: "The datasets analysed during the current study are available in the GitHub repository..." and "The code used during the current study is available in the GitHub repository..."; also "Raman maps are recorded at low laser power for various hotplate temperatures" and "another Raman map is recorded at high laser-power".

inspection_reports.note: PDF file, cannot inspect data content
